attributes.stem: A 42-year-old man presents to his General Practice clinic with a one-month history of non-pruritic, non-painful skin lesions over his face and many other parts of his body. He has not had these before. He denies fever or recent overseas travel. He works as a sex worker and has multiple sexual partners. His last sexual transmitted infection bloods are pending. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Multiple small skin lesions are visualised on his face, trunk, arms, and genitals (Image). His palms and soles are spared.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely diagnosis in this case is molluscum contagiosum, a common childhood disease caused by the pox virus molluscum contagiosum. This viral infection is characterised by small (2-5 mm), white, brown or pink, dome-shaped papules with a central umbilication. In adults, it is often sexually transmitted and can be widespread, typically sparing the palms and soles as seen in this patient. The patient's occupation as a sex worker puts him at higher risk for sexually transmitted infections, including molluscum contagiosum. Immunosuppression is a well recognised factor in cases of widespread molluscum contagiosum in adults. In this particular case, HIV infection is a potential cause. Secondary infection may complicate the diffuse papules and may take longer to resolve.

Nonetheless, molluscum contagiosum is a common infection in young children under 10 years of age - these are often spread when swimming or sharing towels (indirect contact). Diagnosis relies on spot diagnosis though skin biopsy can be done if there is concerns. Treatment may include cryotherapy, laser ablation, podophyllotoxin cream or taping. The papules will often self-resolve within two years thus treatment may not always be warranted.

Genital warts, caused by human papillomavirus (HPV), typically present as flesh-colored papules or cauliflower-like growths, primarily in the anogenital area. They lack the characteristic central umbilication seen in molluscum contagiosum and are usually confined to the genital region, not widespread on the body.

Scabies is caused by the mite Sarcoptes scabiei and presents with intense itching, especially at night, leading to signs of pruiritis rather than papules. The lack of itching and the appearance of the lesions make scabies less likely in this case.

Milia also presents as benign, non-itchy multiple small pearly-white bumps and can occur anywhere on the body but especially on the face. In adults it can also present as milia en plaque with multiple lesions in a cluster or in response to trauma to the skin. However they lack the classical umbillication that is demonstrated in the case and so is less likely.

Syphilis in its secondary stage can present with a widespread rash, but it typically involves the palms and soles, which are spared in this patient. The rash of secondary syphilis is usually macular or papular and lacks the central umbilication characteristic of molluscum contagiosum.
learningPoints: Molluscum contagiosum characteristically presents as small, flesh-coloured, dome-shaped papules with central umbilication.
In adults, molluscum contagiosum is often sexually transmitted and can be widespread, but sparing palms and soles.
HIV status should be considered in adults with extensive molluscum contagiosum lesions.
references: Oakley A, Vanousova D. Molluscum contagiosum. In: Dermnet NZ [online]. 2021. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/molluscum-contagiosum

Meza-Romero R, Navarrete-Dechent C, Downey C. Molluscum contagiosum: an update and review of new perspectives in etiology, diagnosis, and treatment. Clin Cosmet Investig Dermatol. 2019 May 30;12:373-381. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6553952/

Braue A, Ross G, Varigos G, Kelly H. Epidemiology and impact of childhood molluscum contagiosum: a case series and critical review of the literature. Pediatr Dermatol. 2005;22(4):287-94. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16060861/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
